risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Families were stratified by school district and randomly assigned to one of three conditions... Because of the small sample size, we used an urn randomization procedure to balance the groups on variables that might influence intervention outcomes..." Comment: The study explicitly reports random assignment and specifies an urn randomization procedure with stratification by school district, which indicates a systematic random allocation method rather than a non-random or predictable one.

Quote: "Families were stratified by school district and randomly assigned to one of three conditions... Because of the small sample size, we used an urn randomization procedure to balance the groups..." Comment: The report describes random assignment and an urn randomization procedure, but it does not state any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, opaque sealed envelopes, or identical containers). Therefore, the person enrolling participants could not be judged as protected from foreknowledge of the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Families in the SFP and MSFP conditions were told that one condition included more activities to help parents cope with the stress of having an adolescent, but they did not know whether they were assigned to that condition or not." Comment: This indicates participants were not fully blinded to the existence of different intervention content, and the study does not state that facilitators/personnel delivering the interventions were blinded. Because the outcomes were primarily self-reported parenting and relationship measures, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence responses and behavior, so performance bias is high overall.

Quote: "Pre and post-intervention surveys were mailed to the homes of all families that agreed to participate in the study. Youth and parent surveys were mailed separately" and "Therefore, this study focused only on maternal and youth reports of parenting behavior and relationship quality." Comment: Outcome assessment was by maternal and youth self-report (the participants themselves were the assessors), and the paper does not report participant blinding at assessment. Because the key outcomes are subjective questionnaire measures of parenting behavior and relationship quality, knowledge of allocation could readily influence reporting. The study also reports a mix of mother- and youth-reported subjective outcomes, so lack of blinding makes the detection bias domain high risk.

Quote: "Five of the 65 families in our sample did not complete assessments prior to the beginning of the intervention, and 24 families did not complete assessments after the end of the intervention." Comment: Post-intervention attrition was 24/65 = 36.9% overall, which crosses the strict >=30% threshold for High risk. Although the authors state that "multiple imputation procedures were used" and that they aimed "to conduct analyses based on all families in the sample," ITT/multiple imputation does not override this level of missingness unless there is a fully convincing missing-data analysis with sensitivity analyses; none is reported here. The paper also notes that "fathers were less likely to participate in the research component," indicating additional missingness, but the main reason for High risk is the large overall loss at the relevant post-intervention time point.
